title: Neural Networks from First Principles
text:
0.1 Why this book exists
Neural networks are often taught as software tools before they are taught as mathematical objects.
That creates a problem: many learners can run code but cannot explain what each weight, activation, gradient, and update is doing.
This book takes the opposite approach.
We start from first principles and build neural networks in a way that can be understood by hand, checked line by line, and verified with small code examples.

title: Neural Networks from First Principles
text:
0.2 What a neural network is
A neural network is a mathematical function that maps inputs to outputs through weighted computations.
At the most basic level, a neural network does three things:

takes in numbers,
transforms them using weights, biases, and activation functions,
adjusts those weights and biases so that its predictions become better.

At the smallest scale, a neuron computes a score such as
\[
z = w_1 x_1 + w_2 x_2 + b
\]
and then transforms it into an output such as
\[
\hat{y} = f(z)
\]
where:

\(x_1, x_2\) are input variables,
\(w_1, w_2\) are weights,
\(b\) is a bias,
\(z\) is the pre-activation value,
\(f(\cdot)\) is an activation function,
\(\hat{y}\) is the prediction.

This small computation is the core building block for everything that follows in the book.

title: Neural Networks from First Principles
section: 0.3 Why we learn by hand first
text:
0.3 Why we learn by hand first
A learner who can compute one full forward pass and one full backward pass by hand understands the real mechanics of training.
That matters because it helps answer questions such as:

Why does the prediction change?
Why does the loss go down?
Why does the gradient have this sign?
Why do we multiply by the derivative of the activation function?
Why do some activations fit regression while others fit classification?

When the numbers are small enough to compute by hand, the logic becomes visible.

title: Neural Networks from First Principles
text:
0.4 What this book covers
This mini-project book teaches:

the basic structure of a neural network,
forward propagation,
loss functions,
backpropagation,
weight updates,
a regression example,
a classification example,
practice exercises with hand-calculable data.

The goal is not to cover everything in deep learning.
The goal is to make the basic logic so clear that later complexity becomes easier to understand.

title: Neural Networks from First Principles
text:
0.5 Who this book is for
This book is designed for:

beginners who want a slow and clear introduction,
students who want to understand the mathematics behind neural-network training,
lecturers who want board-friendly examples,
self-learners who prefer explanation before abstraction,
anyone who wants to understand both regression and classification through one-neuron examples first.

title: Neural Networks from First Principles
text:
0.6 How to use this book
Use this book slowly.
For each worked example:

read the definitions,
write down the symbols,
compute the intermediate values by hand,
compare your results with the code output,
explain in words why each step happened.

Do not rush to code before the arithmetic makes sense.
Keep a notebook beside you.
The book becomes much more useful when you write while reading.

0.8 Chapter roadmap
The book is organized as follows:

Basics of Neural Networks — definitions, symbols, and intuition.
Single Neuron Regression — a first hand-calculable prediction problem.
Single Neuron Classification — a first hand-calculable classification problem.
Backpropagation for Regression — step-by-step gradient calculations for regression.
Backpropagation for Classification — sigmoid plus cross-entropy backpropagation.
Practice Exercises — learner drills and checks.
Glossary — key terms and symbols.
References — supporting sources.

title: Neural Networks from First Principles
section: 0.10 What you should be able to do by the end
text:
0.10 What you should be able to do by the end
By the end of this book, you should be able to:

explain what a neuron computes,
perform a regression forward pass by hand,
perform a classification forward pass by hand,
compute squared error loss,
compute binary cross-entropy loss,
trace a gradient backward,
update parameters using gradient descent,
explain the difference between regression and classification outputs.

title: Neural Networks from First Principles
section: 0.11 What this book does not try to do
text:
0.11 What this book does not try to do
This book does not try to cover:

deep architectures,
convolutional networks,
transformers,
optimization tricks,
large-scale training systems.

Those topics matter, but they make much more sense after the one-neuron learning logic is clear.

title: Neural Networks from First Principles
section: 0.12 A note to learners and lecturers
text:
0.12 A note to learners and lecturers
This book is written as both a learning guide and a teaching reference.
Learners can use it for careful self-study.
Lecturers can use it for classroom demonstrations, board walkthroughs, hand-calculation exercises, and conceptual reinforcement before coding labs.

title: Neural Networks from First Principles
text:
0.13 Study habit recommendation
For every chapter:

redraw the neuron,
label each symbol,
compute each intermediate quantity,
write one sentence explaining why that step exists.

That habit turns formulas into understanding.

title: 2  About This Book
text:
2.1 Why I wrote this book
I wrote this book while studying Machine Learning at BI Norwegian Business School.
During that work, I became increasingly aware that many learners could follow software steps without fully understanding the mathematical logic underneath them.
I saw a need for a slower, clearer, first-principles explanation that would help fellow students and other beginners understand what a neuron computes, how loss is defined, and how backpropagation works step by step.
This book was written to meet that need.
Its aim is to make the foundational mechanics of neural networks visible, hand-calculable, and teachable before moving to larger software frameworks and more complex architectures.

title: 3  Basics of Neural Networks
text:
3.1 Why this chapter exists
Before we train a neural network, we must know what its basic parts are.
A neural network is not magic. It is a sequence of numerical operations, and each operation has a purpose. If we understand the parts clearly, then forward propagation and backpropagation become easier to follow.
This chapter defines the basic objects and symbols that will be used throughout the book.

title: 3  Basics of Neural Networks
text:
3.2 The central idea
A neural network takes input values, combines them with adjustable parameters, and produces an output.
During training, those adjustable parameters are changed so that predictions become better.
At the smallest scale, the core object is a neuron.

title: 3  Basics of Neural Networks
text:
3.3 What is a neuron?
A neuron is a computational unit that:

receives one or more inputs,
multiplies each input by a weight,
adds the results together,
adds a bias,
optionally applies an activation function.

This produces either a prediction or an intermediate transformed value.

title: 3  Basics of Neural Networks
text:
3.4 Symbols and meanings
We will use the following symbols throughout the book.



Symbol
Meaning
Plain-language meaning




\(x\)
input
information given to the model


\(w\)
weight
how strongly an input matters


\(b\)
bias
a shift term added before activation


\(z\)
pre-activation
weighted sum before activation


\(f(\cdot)\)
activation function
transformation applied to \(z\)


\(a\)
activation output
the output after activation


\(\hat{y}\)
prediction
the model’s estimated output


\(y\)
true target
the correct answer


\(L\)
loss
a number measuring prediction error


\(\eta\)
learning rate
step size in gradient descent

title: 3  Basics of Neural Networks
text:
3.5 A single-neuron formula
For a neuron with two inputs, we can write:
\[
z = w_1 x_1 + w_2 x_2 + b
\]
Then the neuron output is:
\[
a = f(z)
\]
In some settings, we also write the prediction as:
\[
\hat{y} = a
\]
So the neuron first computes a weighted sum and then transforms it.

title: 3  Basics of Neural Networks
text:
3.6 Why weights exist
Weights tell the network how important each input is.
If a weight is large and positive, then increasing that input tends to increase the neuron’s pre-activation value.
If a weight is negative, then increasing that input tends to decrease the neuron’s pre-activation value.
So a weight is not just a number. It expresses directional influence.

title: 3  Basics of Neural Networks
text:
3.7 Why bias exists
The bias allows the neuron to shift its decision rule or prediction rule.
Without the bias, the weighted sum would always be forced through the origin in a simple linear setting. The bias adds flexibility.
You can think of the bias as a starting offset.

title: 3  Basics of Neural Networks
text:
3.8 What an activation function does
An activation function takes the pre-activation value \(z\) and transforms it.
Why do this?
Because we often want the neuron output to have a particular shape or interpretation.
Examples:

in regression, we may use the identity activation:
\[
f(z) = z
\]
in binary classification, we often use the sigmoid activation:
\[
f(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}
\]

The activation function helps determine the kind of prediction the network makes.
These ideas are standard in introductory neural-network treatments (Bishop, 2006; Goodfellow et al., 2016; Haykin, 2009).

title: 3  Basics of Neural Networks
text:
3.9 Identity activation
The identity activation does nothing to the input:
\[
f(z) = z
\]
So the output becomes:
\[
a = z
\]
This is common in regression when we want the prediction to be any real number.

title: 3  Basics of Neural Networks
text:
3.10 Sigmoid activation
The sigmoid function is:
\[
f(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}
\]
This maps any real number into the interval \((0, 1)\).
That makes it useful for binary classification, where we want the output to behave like a probability.

title: 3  Basics of Neural Networks
text:
3.11 Forward propagation: the big idea
Forward propagation means computing the prediction from the current inputs and current parameter values.
In the simplest case, the process is:

start with the inputs,
multiply each input by its weight,
add the products,
add the bias,
apply the activation function,
obtain the prediction.

So forward propagation is the act of moving from input to output.

title: 3  Basics of Neural Networks
text:
3.12 A tiny numeric example
Suppose:
\[
x_1 = 2, \quad x_2 = 3, \quad w_1 = 0.5, \quad w_2 = -1, \quad b = 0.25
\]
Then:
\[
z = w_1 x_1 + w_2 x_2 + b
\]
Substitute the numbers:
\[
z = (0.5)(2) + (-1)(3) + 0.25
\]
Compute each part:
\[
(0.5)(2) = 1
\]
\[
(-1)(3) = -3
\]
So:
\[
z = 1 - 3 + 0.25 = -1.75
\]
If we use the identity activation, then:
\[
a = z = -1.75
\]
If we use sigmoid, then:
\[
a = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-(-1.75)}} = \frac{1}{1 + e^{1.75}}
\]
So the same pre-activation value can produce different final outputs depending on the activation function.

title: 3  Basics of Neural Networks
text:
3.13 Why loss is needed
A neural network must know how wrong its prediction is.
That is the job of the loss function.
The loss function compares:

the prediction \(\hat{y}\),
the true answer \(y\).

If the prediction is close to the true answer, the loss is small.
If the prediction is far away, the loss is larger.
Training tries to reduce loss.

title: 3  Basics of Neural Networks
text:
3.14 Why gradients are needed
If loss tells us how bad the prediction is, the gradient tells us how to change the weights and bias to improve it.
A gradient answers questions such as:

If I increase \(w_1\) a little, what happens to the loss?
If I decrease \(b\) a little, does the loss improve?

This is why gradients are central to learning.

title: 3  Basics of Neural Networks
text:
3.15 The training loop in words
A simple training cycle is:

compute the prediction,
compute the loss,
compute gradients,
update parameters,
repeat.

This cycle is the heart of neural-network learning.

title: 3  Basics of Neural Networks
text:
3.16 Common mistakes

3.16.1 Mistake 1: confusing \(z\) and \(a\)

\(z\) is the value before activation.
\(a\) is the value after activation.

These are not always the same.
They are the same only when the activation is the identity function.


3.16.2 Mistake 2: forgetting the bias
A learner may compute:
\[
z = w_1 x_1 + w_2 x_2
\]
and forget the \(+ b\) term.
That changes the result.


3.16.3 Mistake 3: using sigmoid for unrestricted regression output
Sigmoid forces outputs into \((0, 1)\), so it is usually not suitable when regression targets can be any real number.


3.16.4 Mistake 4: thinking the neuron “understands”
A neuron does not understand. It computes.
The apparent intelligence comes from repeated parameter adjustment across many examples.

title: 3  Basics of Neural Networks
text:
3.17 Chapter checklist
By the end of this chapter, you should be able to answer:

What is a weight?
What is a bias?
What is the difference between \(z\) and \(a\)?
What does an activation function do?
Why is sigmoid useful for binary classification?
Why do we need a loss function?
Why do we need gradients?

title: 3  Basics of Neural Networks
text:
3.18 Suggested by-hand exercise
Using:
\[
x_1 = 1,\quad x_2 = 4,\quad w_1 = 2,\quad w_2 = -0.5,\quad b = 1
\]
compute:

the pre-activation value \(z\),
the output using identity activation,
the output using sigmoid activation.

Write every intermediate step by hand before checking with code.




Bishop, C. M. (2006). Pattern recognition and machine learning. Springer.


Goodfellow, I., Bengio, Y., & Courville, A. (2016). Deep learning. MIT Press. https://www.deeplearningbook.org/


Haykin, S. (2009). Neural networks and learning machines (3rd ed.). Pearson.

title: 4  Single Neuron Regression
text:
4.1 Why this chapter exists
This chapter introduces the simplest neural-network prediction setting: a single neuron used for regression.
Regression means we want to predict a numerical value, such as:
We begin here because regression with one neuron is the clearest place to learn the mechanics of forward computation.

title: 4  Single Neuron Regression
text:
house price,
temperature,
sales,
exam score,
energy usage.

title: 4  Single Neuron Regression
text:
4.2 What regression means
A regression problem predicts a numerical target.
If the true target is \(y = 8.5\), then a prediction such as \(\hat{y} = 8.2\) or \(\hat{y} = 9.1\) is still meaningful, because both are numerical values.
In this chapter, the neuron’s output is allowed to be any real number.
That is why we use the identity activation:
\[
f(z) = z
\]
So the neuron’s output is simply:
\[
\hat{y} = z
\]

title: 4  Single Neuron Regression
text:
4.3 The model
We use a neuron with two inputs:
\[
z = w_1 x_1 + w_2 x_2 + b
\]
Because we are using identity activation, the prediction is:
\[
\hat{y} = z
\]
So the full model is:
\[
\hat{y} = w_1 x_1 + w_2 x_2 + b
\]

title: 4  Single Neuron Regression
text:
4.4 Our tiny dataset
We use a very small dataset with two input variables and one target.



Example
\(x_1\)
\(x_2\)
\(y\)




1
1
2
4


2
2
1
5


3
3
0
6



This dataset is small enough to compute by hand.

title: 4  Single Neuron Regression
text:
4.5 Why this dataset is useful
This dataset is intentionally tiny.
It helps us focus on the mechanics of the neuron instead of getting lost in too many numbers.
A small dataset is ideal when learning:

weighted sums,
predictions,
errors,
parameter effects.

title: 4  Single Neuron Regression
text:
4.6 Initial parameter values
We must start with some parameter values.
Suppose:
\[
w_1 = 0.5, \quad w_2 = 1.0, \quad b = 0.0
\]
These are not yet learned values. They are just starting values.

title: 4  Single Neuron Regression
text:
4.7 Forward pass for Example 1
Take Example 1:
\[
x_1 = 1,\quad x_2 = 2,\quad y = 4
\]
The model is:
\[
\hat{y} = w_1 x_1 + w_2 x_2 + b
\]
Substitute the values:
\[
\hat{y} = (0.5)(1) + (1.0)(2) + 0.0
\]
Compute each term:
\[
(0.5)(1) = 0.5
\]
\[
(1.0)(2) = 2.0
\]
Add them:
\[
\hat{y} = 0.5 + 2.0 + 0.0 = 2.5
\]
So the prediction is:
\[
\hat{y} = 2.5
\]

title: 4  Single Neuron Regression
text:
4.8 Interpreting the prediction
The true target is:
\[
y = 4
\]
But the model predicted:
\[
\hat{y} = 2.5
\]
So the prediction is too low by:
\[
y - \hat{y} = 4 - 2.5 = 1.5
\]
This means the current parameters are not yet giving a good prediction for this example.

title: 4  Single Neuron Regression
text:
4.9 Loss for one example
A common regression loss is the squared error:
\[
L = \frac{1}{2}(y - \hat{y})^2
\]
Why include the factor \(\frac{1}{2}\)?
Because later, when differentiating, it simplifies the derivative.
For Example 1:
\[
L = \frac{1}{2}(4 - 2.5)^2
\]
First compute the error:
\[
4 - 2.5 = 1.5
\]
Square it:
\[
(1.5)^2 = 2.25
\]
Multiply by \(\frac{1}{2}\):
\[
L = \frac{1}{2}(2.25) = 1.125
\]
So the loss for Example 1 is:
\[
L = 1.125
\]

title: 4  Single Neuron Regression
text:
4.10 Why squared error is useful
Squared error does two important things:

it penalizes larger errors more strongly,
it makes the loss always nonnegative.

It is also mathematically convenient when we derive gradients.

title: 4  Single Neuron Regression
section: 4.11 Forward pass for all three examples

title: 4  Single Neuron Regression
text:
4.12 Compare predictions with targets



Example
Target \(y\)
Prediction \(\hat{y}\)
Error \(y - \hat{y}\)




1
4.0
2.5
1.5


2
5.0
2.0
3.0

These errors are large, which tells us the initial parameters are poor.
That is normal at the start of training.

title: 4  Single Neuron Regression
text:
4.13 Mean loss over the dataset
For each example, use:
\[
L_i = \frac{1}{2}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2
\]

4.13.1 Example 1 loss
\[
L_1 = \frac{1}{2}(4 - 2.5)^2 = \frac{1}{2}(1.5)^2 = 1.125
\]


4.13.2 Example 2 loss
\[
L_2 = \frac{1}{2}(5 - 2.0)^2 = \frac{1}{2}(3.0)^2 = 4.5
\]


4.13.3 Example 3 loss
\[
L_3 = \frac{1}{2}(6 - 1.5)^2 = \frac{1}{2}(4.5)^2 = 10.125
\]
Now compute the average:
\[
\text{Mean Loss} = \frac{L_1 + L_2 + L_3}{3}
\]
Substitute:
\[
\text{Mean Loss} = \frac{1.125 + 4.5 + 10.125}{3}
\]
\[
\text{Mean Loss} = \frac{15.75}{3} = 5.25
\]
So the mean loss over the dataset is:
\[
\text{Mean Loss} = 5.25
\]

title: 4  Single Neuron Regression
section: 4.14 What the loss is telling us
text:
4.14 What the loss is telling us
The loss is not just a number.
It summarizes model performance under the current parameters.
A large loss means:

the predictions are far from the targets,
the current weights and bias need adjustment.

title: 4  Single Neuron Regression
text:
4.15 A small Python check

# Tiny regression example: forward pass and squared-error loss

data = [
    {"x1": 1, "x2": 2, "y": 4},
    {"x1": 2, "x2": 1, "y": 5},
    {"x1": 3, "x2": 0, "y": 6},
]

w1 = 0.5
w2 = 1.0
b = 0.0

losses = []

for i, row in enumerate(data, start=1):
    y_hat = w1 * row["x1"] + w2 * row["x2"] + b
    loss = 0.5 * (row["y"] - y_hat) ** 2
    losses.append(loss)

    print(f"Example {i}")
    print(f"  x1 = {row['x1']}, x2 = {row['x2']}, y = {row['y']}")
    print(f"  prediction = {y_hat}")
    print(f"  loss = {loss}")
    print()

mean_loss = sum(losses) / len(losses)
print(f"Mean loss = {mean_loss}")

Example 1
  x1 = 1, x2 = 2, y = 4
  prediction = 2.5
  loss = 1.125

Example 2
  x1 = 2, x2 = 1, y = 5
  prediction = 2.0
  loss = 4.5

Example 3
  x1 = 3, x2 = 0, y = 6
  prediction = 1.5
  loss = 10.125

Mean loss = 5.25

title: 4  Single Neuron Regression
text:
4.16 Why this code matters
The code does not replace understanding.
It only checks the hand calculations.
The correct learning order is:

compute by hand,
predict what the code should output,
run the code,
compare the results.

title: 4  Single Neuron Regression
text:
4.17 Common mistakes

4.17.1 Mistake 1: forgetting that identity activation changes nothing
If:
\[
f(z) = z
\]
then:
\[
\hat{y} = z
\]
There is no extra transformation.


4.17.2 Mistake 2: mixing up error and loss
The error may be:
\[
y - \hat{y}
\]
But the loss here is:
\[
\frac{1}{2}(y - \hat{y})^2
\]
These are not the same quantity.


4.17.3 Mistake 3: forgetting to square the error
If the error is \(3\), then the squared part is \(9\), not \(3\).


4.17.4 Mistake 4: forgetting the factor \(\frac{1}{2}\)
In this chapter, we are defining loss as:
\[
L = \frac{1}{2}(y - \hat{y})^2
\]
So keep the factor.

title: 4  Single Neuron Regression
text:
4.18 Chapter checklist
By the end of this chapter, you should be able to answer:

What makes this a regression problem?
Why do we use identity activation here?
How do we compute \(\hat{y}\) for one example?
How do we compute squared error loss?
What does a large loss mean?
Why do we check hand calculations with code?

title: 4  Single Neuron Regression
text:
4.19 Suggested by-hand exercise
Using the same dataset, but with:
\[
w_1 = 1,\quad w_2 = 1,\quad b = 0
\]
compute by hand:

the prediction for each example,
the loss for each example,
the mean loss across all examples.

Then compare your answers with a short Python script.

title: 5  Single Neuron Classification
text:
5.1 Why this chapter exists
This chapter introduces the simplest neural-network classification setting: a single neuron used for binary classification.
Binary classification means there are only two possible classes, such as:
Unlike regression, classification usually needs an output that can be interpreted as a probability-like quantity.
That is why we do not use the identity activation here.

title: 5  Single Neuron Classification
text:
0 or 1,
no or yes,
fail or pass,
negative or positive.

title: 5  Single Neuron Classification
text:
5.2 What binary classification means
In binary classification, the target variable takes one of two values:
\[
y \in \{0,1\}
\]
For example:

\(y = 0\) may mean “did not buy”,
\(y = 1\) may mean “did buy”.

The model should output a number that tells us how strongly it leans toward class 1.
So we want the prediction to lie between 0 and 1.

title: 5  Single Neuron Classification
text:
5.3 Why sigmoid is used
The sigmoid activation function is:
\[
\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}
\]
This takes any real number \(z\) and maps it into the interval:
\[
(0,1)
\]
That makes sigmoid useful for binary classification.
If the output is close to 1, the model is leaning toward class 1.
If the output is close to 0, the model is leaning toward class 0.

title: 5  Single Neuron Classification
text:
5.4 The model
We again use a neuron with two inputs:
\[
z = w_1 x_1 + w_2 x_2 + b
\]
But now the prediction is:
\[
\hat{y} = \sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}
\]
So the model is no longer just a linear output. It is a linear score followed by sigmoid.

title: 5  Single Neuron Classification
text:
5.5 Our tiny classification dataset
We use this small dataset:



Example
\(x_1\)
\(x_2\)
\(y\)




1
0
0
0


2
0
1
0


3
1
0
1


4
1
1
1



This is small enough for hand calculation and helps us see how the neuron behaves.

title: 5  Single Neuron Classification
text:
5.6 Why this dataset is useful
This dataset is useful because it contains both classes and keeps the arithmetic manageable.
We can inspect:

how the weighted sum changes,
how sigmoid transforms that sum,
how the predicted probability responds.

title: 5  Single Neuron Classification
text:
5.7 Initial parameter values
Suppose we start with:
\[
w_1 = 2,\quad w_2 = -1,\quad b = -0.5
\]
These are just starting values.

title: 5  Single Neuron Classification
text:
5.8 Forward pass for Example 1
Take Example 1:
\[
x_1 = 0,\quad x_2 = 0,\quad y = 0
\]
First compute the pre-activation:
\[
z = w_1 x_1 + w_2 x_2 + b
\]
Substitute:
\[
z = (2)(0) + (-1)(0) + (-0.5)
\]
\[
z = 0 + 0 - 0.5 = -0.5
\]
Now apply sigmoid:
\[
\hat{y} = \sigma(-0.5) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{0.5}}
\]
Using the approximation:
\[
e^{0.5} \approx 1.6487
\]
we get:
\[
\hat{y} \approx \frac{1}{1 + 1.6487}
= \frac{1}{2.6487}
\approx 0.3775
\]
So the predicted probability-like output is:
\[
\hat{y} \approx 0.3775
\]

title: 5  Single Neuron Classification
text:
5.9 Interpreting the prediction
The true target is:
\[
y = 0
\]
The model predicted:
\[
\hat{y} \approx 0.3775
\]
This is below 0.5, so the model leans toward class 0.
That is directionally reasonable, although not extremely confident.

title: 5  Single Neuron Classification
text:
5.10 Decision rule
A common decision rule is:

predict class 1 if \(\hat{y} \ge 0.5\),
predict class 0 if \(\hat{y} < 0.5\).

This threshold is common, though not always mandatory.
For Example 1:
\[
0.3775 < 0.5
\]
so the predicted class is 0.

title: 5  Single Neuron Classification
text:
5.11 Binary cross-entropy loss
For binary classification, a common loss is binary cross-entropy:
\[
L = -\left[y \log(\hat{y}) + (1-y)\log(1-\hat{y})\right]
\]
This loss behaves sensibly for probabilities:

if the model assigns high probability to the correct class, the loss is small,
if the model assigns low probability to the correct class, the loss is large.

title: 5  Single Neuron Classification
text:
5.12 Loss for Example 1
For Example 1, we have:
\[
y = 0,\quad \hat{y} \approx 0.3775
\]
Substitute into the loss:
\[
L = -\left[0\cdot \log(0.3775) + (1-0)\log(1-0.3775)\right]
\]
Simplify:
\[
L = -\log(0.6225)
\]
Using:
\[
\log(0.6225) \approx -0.4741
\]
we get:
\[
L \approx 0.4741
\]
So the loss is:
\[
L \approx 0.4741
\]

title: 5  Single Neuron Classification
section: 5.13 Forward pass for all four examples

title: 5  Single Neuron Classification
text:
5.14 Compare predicted probabilities and classes
Using the threshold 0.5:



Example
True \(y\)
\(\hat{y}\)
Predicted class




1

3

In this setup, all four classes are predicted correctly.
That does not mean the model is perfect. It only means it performs well on this tiny example.

title: 5  Single Neuron Classification
text:
5.15 Why the loss still matters
Even when the predicted class is correct, the probability may still be weak.
For example, predicting
\[
\hat{y} = 0.51
\]
for a true class of 1 is correct by threshold, but not very confident.
Loss captures not just correctness, but also confidence.

title: 5  Single Neuron Classification
text:
5.16 A small Python check

import math

data = [
    {"x1": 0, "x2": 0, "y": 0},
    {"x1": 0, "x2": 1, "y": 0},
    {"x1": 1, "x2": 0, "y": 1},
    {"x1": 1, "x2": 1, "y": 1},
]

w1 = 2.0
w2 = -1.0
b = -0.5

def sigmoid(z):
    return 1 / (1 + math.exp(-z))

for i, row in enumerate(data, start=1):
    z = w1 * row["x1"] + w2 * row["x2"] + b
    y_hat = sigmoid(z)
    pred_class = 1 if y_hat >= 0.5 else 0
    loss = -(row["y"] * math.log(y_hat) + (1 - row["y"]) * math.log(1 - y_hat))

    print(f"Example {i}")
    print(f"  x1 = {row['x1']}, x2 = {row['x2']}, y = {row['y']}")
    print(f"  z = {z}")
    print(f"  prediction = {y_hat:.4f}")
    print(f"  predicted class = {pred_class}")
    print(f"  loss = {loss:.4f}")
    print()

Example 1
  x1 = 0, x2 = 0, y = 0
  z = -0.5
  prediction = 0.3775
  predicted class = 0
  loss = 0.4741

Example 2
  x1 = 0, x2 = 1, y = 0
  z = -1.5
  prediction = 0.1824
  predicted class = 0
  loss = 0.2014

Example 3
  x1 = 1, x2 = 0, y = 1
  z = 1.5
  prediction = 0.8176
  predicted class = 1
  loss = 0.2014

Example 4
  x1 = 1, x2 = 1, y = 1
  z = 0.5
  prediction = 0.6225
  predicted class = 1
  loss = 0.4741

title: 5  Single Neuron Classification
text:
5.17 Why this code matters
Again, the code is a check, not a substitute for reasoning.
The learner should first ask:

What do I expect \(z\) to be?
What do I expect sigmoid to do to that value?
Should the output be above or below 0.5?

Then the code confirms the calculation.

title: 5  Single Neuron Classification
text:
5.18 Common mistakes

5.18.1 Mistake 1: using identity activation for binary classification
Identity output can be any real number, such as 3.2 or -4.7.
That is not suitable when we want a probability-like output.


5.18.2 Mistake 2: thinking sigmoid output is a guaranteed true probability
It is better to say it is a probability-like score produced by the model.
Whether it is well calibrated is a separate question.


5.18.3 Mistake 3: confusing the class prediction with the probability output
These are different:

\(\hat{y} = 0.8176\) is the model output,
class 1 is the thresholded decision.



5.18.4 Mistake 4: forgetting that cross-entropy uses logarithms
Binary cross-entropy is not the same as squared error.
Its form is specifically designed for probabilistic binary outputs.

title: 5  Single Neuron Classification
text:
5.19 Chapter checklist
By the end of this chapter, you should be able to answer:

Why do we use sigmoid for binary classification?
Why must the output lie between 0 and 1?
How do we compute \(\hat{y}\) from \(z\)?
How do we convert a probability-like output into a class?
Why can the class be correct even when the loss is not tiny?
Why is cross-entropy useful here?

title: 5  Single Neuron Classification
text:
5.20 Suggested by-hand exercise
Using the same dataset, but with:
\[
w_1 = 1,\quad w_2 = 1,\quad b = -0.5
\]
compute by hand for all four examples:

the pre-activation value \(z\),
the sigmoid output \(\hat{y}\),
the predicted class using threshold 0.5.

Then compare with a short Python script.

title: 6  Backpropagation for Regression
text:
6.1 Why this chapter exists
A neural network becomes useful only when it can improve its parameters.
That means it must answer this question:
How should each weight and bias change so that the loss becomes smaller?
Backpropagation is the method used to compute these needed changes.
This chapter explains backpropagation from first principles using the simplest possible setting:

title: 6  Backpropagation for Regression
text:
one neuron,
two inputs,
identity activation,
squared error loss,
one training example.

title: 6  Backpropagation for Regression
text:
6.2 What backpropagation means
Backpropagation is the process of computing how the loss changes with respect to each parameter.
In symbols, we want quantities such as:
\[
\frac{\partial L}{\partial w_1}, \quad \frac{\partial L}{\partial w_2}, \quad \frac{\partial L}{\partial b}
\]
These are called gradients.
They tell us how sensitive the loss is to each parameter.

title: 6  Backpropagation for Regression
text:
6.3 Why gradients matter
Suppose a gradient is large and positive:
\[
\frac{\partial L}{\partial w_1} > 0
\]
That means increasing \(w_1\) would increase the loss locally.
So to reduce loss, we would usually move \(w_1\) downward.
If the gradient is negative, the opposite idea applies.
So the gradient is a direction signal.

title: 6  Backpropagation for Regression
text:
6.4 Our setup
We use the same single-neuron regression form:
\[
z = w_1 x_1 + w_2 x_2 + b
\]
Because we are using identity activation:
\[
\hat{y} = z
\]
The loss for one example is:
\[
L = \frac{1}{2}(y - \hat{y})^2
\]

title: 6  Backpropagation for Regression
text:
6.5 One training example
Use this single example:
\[
x_1 = 1,\quad x_2 = 2,\quad y = 4
\]
and initial parameters:
\[
w_1 = 0.5,\quad w_2 = 1.0,\quad b = 0.0
\]

title: 6  Backpropagation for Regression
text:
6.6 Step 1: forward pass
First compute the prediction.
\[
\hat{y} = w_1 x_1 + w_2 x_2 + b
\]
Substitute:
\[
\hat{y} = (0.5)(1) + (1.0)(2) + 0
\]
\[
\hat{y} = 0.5 + 2.0 = 2.5
\]
So:
\[
\hat{y} = 2.5
\]
Now compute the loss:
\[
L = \frac{1}{2}(4 - 2.5)^2
\]
\[
L = \frac{1}{2}(1.5)^2 = \frac{1}{2}(2.25) = 1.125
\]
So the current loss is:
\[
L = 1.125
\]

title: 6  Backpropagation for Regression
text:
6.7 Step 2: identify the dependency chain
To compute gradients, we ask:
How does the loss depend on each parameter?
The dependency chain is:
\[
w_1, w_2, b \to \hat{y} \to L
\]
That means:

the parameters affect the prediction,
the prediction affects the loss.

So we use the chain rule.

title: 6  Backpropagation for Regression
section: 6.8 Step 3: derivative of loss with respect to prediction
text:
6.8 Step 3: derivative of loss with respect to prediction
We start from:
\[
L = \frac{1}{2}(y - \hat{y})^2
\]
Differentiate with respect to \(\hat{y}\):
\[
\frac{\partial L}{\partial \hat{y}} = -(y - \hat{y})
\]
This can also be written as:
\[
\frac{\partial L}{\partial \hat{y}} = \hat{y} - y
\]
These two forms are equivalent.
Now substitute:
\[
\frac{\partial L}{\partial \hat{y}} = 2.5 - 4 = -1.5
\]
So:
\[
\frac{\partial L}{\partial \hat{y}} = -1.5
\]

title: 6  Backpropagation for Regression
text:
6.9 Why this sign makes sense
The gradient is negative because the prediction is too small.
If the prediction increases slightly, the loss should decrease.
That is why the derivative with respect to \(\hat{y}\) is negative here.

title: 6  Backpropagation for Regression
section: 6.10 Step 4: derivative of prediction with respect to each parameter
text:
6.10 Step 4: derivative of prediction with respect to each parameter
The prediction is:
\[
\hat{y} = w_1 x_1 + w_2 x_2 + b
\]
Treat the inputs as constants.
Then:
\[
\frac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial w_1} = x_1
\]
\[
\frac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial w_2} = x_2
\]
\[
\frac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial b} = 1
\]
Now substitute the input values:
\[
\frac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial w_1} = 1
\]
\[
\frac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial w_2} = 2
\]
\[
\frac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial b} = 1
\]

title: 6  Backpropagation for Regression
text:
6.11 Step 5: apply the chain rule
For each parameter:
\[
\frac{\partial L}{\partial w_1}
=
\frac{\partial L}{\partial \hat{y}}
\cdot
\frac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial w_1}
\]
\[
\frac{\partial L}{\partial w_2}
=
\frac{\partial L}{\partial \hat{y}}
\cdot
\frac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial w_2}
\]
\[
\frac{\partial L}{\partial b}
=
\frac{\partial L}{\partial \hat{y}}
\cdot
\frac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial b}
\]
Substitute the values.

6.11.1 Gradient for \(w_1\)
\[
\frac{\partial L}{\partial w_1} = (-1.5)(1) = -1.5
\]


6.11.2 Gradient for \(w_2\)
\[
\frac{\partial L}{\partial w_2} = (-1.5)(2) = -3.0
\]


6.11.3 Gradient for \(b\)
\[
\frac{\partial L}{\partial b} = (-1.5)(1) = -1.5
\]
So the gradients are:
\[
\frac{\partial L}{\partial w_1} = -1.5,\quad
\frac{\partial L}{\partial w_2} = -3.0,\quad
\frac{\partial L}{\partial b} = -1.5
\]

title: 6  Backpropagation for Regression
section: 6.12 Why these gradient values make sense
text:
6.12 Why these gradient values make sense
Because the prediction is too low, the model needs to increase its output.
The gradients are negative, so subtracting them during gradient descent will increase the parameters.
That will tend to increase the prediction on this example.
This is exactly what we want.

title: 6  Backpropagation for Regression
text:
6.13 Step 6: update the parameters
Gradient descent updates parameters using:
\[
\theta_{\text{new}} = \theta_{\text{old}} - \eta \frac{\partial L}{\partial \theta}
\]
where \(\eta\) is the learning rate.
Let:
\[
\eta = 0.1
\]

6.13.1 Update \(w_1\)
\[
w_1^{\text{new}} = 0.5 - 0.1(-1.5)
\]
\[
w_1^{\text{new}} = 0.5 + 0.15 = 0.65
\]


6.13.2 Update \(w_2\)
\[
w_2^{\text{new}} = 1.0 - 0.1(-3.0)
\]
\[
w_2^{\text{new}} = 1.0 + 0.3 = 1.3
\]


6.13.3 Update \(b\)
\[
b^{\text{new}} = 0.0 - 0.1(-1.5)
\]
\[
b^{\text{new}} = 0.15
\]
So the updated parameters are:
\[
w_1 = 0.65,\quad w_2 = 1.3,\quad b = 0.15
\]

title: 6  Backpropagation for Regression
section: 6.14 Step 7: check whether the loss decreased
text:
6.14 Step 7: check whether the loss decreased
Now compute the new prediction:
\[
\hat{y}_{\text{new}} = (0.65)(1) + (1.3)(2) + 0.15
\]
\[
\hat{y}_{\text{new}} = 0.65 + 2.6 + 0.15 = 3.4
\]
Now compute the new loss:
\[
L_{\text{new}} = \frac{1}{2}(4 - 3.4)^2
\]
\[
L_{\text{new}} = \frac{1}{2}(0.6)^2 = \frac{1}{2}(0.36) = 0.18
\]
So the loss changed from:
\[
1.125 \to 0.18
\]
This is a large improvement after one update step.

title: 6  Backpropagation for Regression
text:
6.15 Why the loss decreased
The prediction moved from:
\[
2.5 \to 3.4
\]
The target was:
\[
4
\]
So the prediction became closer to the target.
Since squared error penalizes distance from the target, the loss decreased.

title: 6  Backpropagation for Regression
text:
6.16 A small Python check

# Backpropagation by hand for one regression example

x1 = 1
x2 = 2
y = 4

w1 = 0.5
w2 = 1.0
b = 0.0
eta = 0.1

# Forward pass
y_hat = w1 * x1 + w2 * x2 + b
loss = 0.5 * (y - y_hat) ** 2

# Backward pass
dL_dyhat = y_hat - y
dyhat_dw1 = x1
dyhat_dw2 = x2
dyhat_db = 1

dL_dw1 = dL_dyhat * dyhat_dw1
dL_dw2 = dL_dyhat * dyhat_dw2
dL_db = dL_dyhat * dyhat_db

# Parameter update
w1_new = w1 - eta * dL_dw1
w2_new = w2 - eta * dL_dw2
b_new = b - eta * dL_db

# New forward pass
y_hat_new = w1_new * x1 + w2_new * x2 + b_new
loss_new = 0.5 * (y - y_hat_new) ** 2

print("Initial prediction:", y_hat)
print("Initial loss:", loss)
print()
print("Gradients:")
print("  dL/dw1 =", dL_dw1)
print("  dL/dw2 =", dL_dw2)
print("  dL/db  =", dL_db)
print()
print("Updated parameters:")
print("  w1 =", w1_new)
print("  w2 =", w2_new)
print("  b  =", b_new)
print()
print("New prediction:", y_hat_new)
print("New loss:", loss_new)

Initial prediction: 2.5
Initial loss: 1.125

Gradients:
  dL/dw1 = -1.5
  dL/dw2 = -3.0
  dL/db  = -1.5

Updated parameters:
  w1 = 0.65
  w2 = 1.3
  b  = 0.15000000000000002

New prediction: 3.4
New loss: 0.18000000000000005

title: 6  Backpropagation for Regression
section: 6.17 Why the chain rule is the heart of backpropagation
text:
6.17 Why the chain rule is the heart of backpropagation
Backpropagation is not a mysterious special trick.
It is repeated use of the chain rule.
Backpropagation is the repeated application of the chain rule through a layered computation (Goodfellow et al., 2016; Haykin, 2009; Rumelhart et al., 1986).
The chain rule lets us move backward through a computation graph and ask:
If this final quantity changed, how much did each earlier quantity contribute?
That is the central logic of learning.

title: 6  Backpropagation for Regression
text:
6.18 Common mistakes

6.18.1 Mistake 1: differentiating with respect to the wrong variable
Be clear about what is changing and what is being held constant.
For example:

\(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \hat{y}}\) is not the same as \(\frac{\partial L}{\partial w_1}\)



6.18.2 Mistake 2: dropping the sign
A sign error changes the direction of learning.
That can make the model worse instead of better.


6.18.3 Mistake 3: forgetting the chain rule
You do not jump directly from \(L\) to \(w_1\) without following the dependency path.


6.18.4 Mistake 4: updating in the wrong direction
Gradient descent subtracts the gradient:
\[
\theta_{\text{new}} = \theta_{\text{old}} - \eta \frac{\partial L}{\partial \theta}
\]
not plus the gradient.

title: 6  Backpropagation for Regression
text:
6.19 Chapter checklist
By the end of this chapter, you should be able to answer:

What does backpropagation compute?
Why do we need gradients?
What is the chain rule doing here?
How do we compute \(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \hat{y}}\)?
How do we compute gradients for \(w_1\), \(w_2\), and \(b\)?
Why did the loss decrease after the update?

title: 6  Backpropagation for Regression
text:
6.20 Suggested by-hand exercise
Use:
\[
x_1 = 2,\quad x_2 = 1,\quad y = 5
\]
Initial parameters:
\[
w_1 = 0.5,\quad w_2 = 1.0,\quad b = 0.0
\]
Learning rate:
\[
\eta = 0.1
\]
Assume the model:
\[
\hat{y} = w_1 x_1 + w_2 x_2 + b
\]
Assume the activation function is the identity function:
\[
f(z) = z
\]
Use the loss:
\[
L = \frac{1}{2}(y - \hat{y})^2
\]
Compute by hand:

the prediction \(\hat{y}\),
the loss \(L\),
\(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \hat{y}}\),
\(\frac{\partial L}{\partial w_1}\),
\(\frac{\partial L}{\partial w_2}\),
\(\frac{\partial L}{\partial b}\),
the updated parameters,
the new prediction and new loss.

title: 6  Backpropagation for Regression
text:
6.21 One important conceptual note
This chapter uses regression backpropagation first, not classification backpropagation.
That is deliberate.
Reason:

sigmoid + cross-entropy backpropagation is extremely important,
but it adds extra derivative complexity,
and the learner should first master the simpler chain-rule case.

The next chapter applies the same backpropagation logic to binary classification.




Goodfellow, I., Bengio, Y., & Courville, A. (2016). Deep learning. MIT Press. https://www.deeplearningbook.org/


Haykin, S. (2009). Neural networks and learning machines (3rd ed.). Pearson.


Rumelhart, D. E., Hinton, G. E., & Williams, R. J. (1986). Learning representations by back-propagating errors. Nature, 323(6088), 533–536. https://doi.org/10.1038/323533a0

title: 7  Backpropagation for Classification
text:
7.1 Why this chapter exists
In the previous chapter, we studied backpropagation for a regression neuron with identity activation.
This chapter extends the same logic to binary classification.
Now the computation has one more important step:
This chapter matters because it shows how learning works when the output is probability-like rather than unrestricted.

title: 7  Backpropagation for Classification
text:
compute the linear score \(z\),
apply sigmoid to obtain \(\hat{y}\),
compute binary cross-entropy loss,
propagate gradients backward,
update the parameters.

title: 7  Backpropagation for Classification
text:
7.2 Our setup
We use one neuron with two inputs:
\[
z = w_1 x_1 + w_2 x_2 + b
\]
The prediction is the sigmoid output:
\[
\hat{y} = \sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}
\]
The loss for one example is binary cross-entropy:
\[
L = -\left[y \log(\hat{y}) + (1-y)\log(1-\hat{y})\right]
\]
This sigmoid-plus-cross-entropy setup is standard in binary neural-network classification (Bishop, 2006; Goodfellow et al., 2016; Haykin, 2009).

title: 7  Backpropagation for Classification
text:
7.3 One training example
Use this example:
\[
x_1 = 1,\quad x_2 = 0,\quad y = 1
\]
and initial parameters:
\[
w_1 = 2,\quad w_2 = -1,\quad b = -0.5
\]
Let the learning rate be:
\[
\eta = 0.1
\]

title: 7  Backpropagation for Classification
text:
7.4 Step 1: forward pass
First compute the linear score:
\[
z = w_1 x_1 + w_2 x_2 + b
\]
Substitute:
\[
z = (2)(1) + (-1)(0) + (-0.5)
\]
\[
z = 2 + 0 - 0.5 = 1.5
\]
Now apply sigmoid:
\[
\hat{y} = \sigma(1.5) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-1.5}}
\]
Using:
\[
e^{-1.5} \approx 0.2231
\]
we get:
\[
\hat{y} \approx \frac{1}{1 + 0.2231}
= \frac{1}{1.2231}
\approx 0.8176
\]
So:
\[
\hat{y} \approx 0.8176
\]

title: 7  Backpropagation for Classification
text:
7.5 Step 2: compute the loss
The true target is:
\[
y = 1
\]
So binary cross-entropy becomes:
\[
L = -\log(\hat{y})
\]
Substitute:
\[
L = -\log(0.8176)
\]
Using:
\[
\log(0.8176) \approx -0.2014
\]
we get:
\[
L \approx 0.2014
\]
So the current loss is:
\[
L \approx 0.2014
\]

title: 7  Backpropagation for Classification
text:
7.6 Step 3: understand the computation chain
Before computing gradients, we must understand how the loss depends on the parameters.
The loss is not a direct function of the weights and bias. Instead, the parameters influence the loss through intermediate quantities.
The sequence of computations is:
\[
(w_1, w_2, b) \rightarrow z \rightarrow \hat{y} \rightarrow L
\]
This means:

the parameters determine the linear score \(z\),
the score determines the prediction \(\hat{y}\),
the prediction determines the loss \(L\).

Written as explicit formulas:

Linear score:
\[
z = w_1 x_1 + w_2 x_2 + b
\]
Sigmoid prediction:
\[
\hat{y} = \sigma(z)
\]
Binary cross-entropy loss:
\[
L = -\left[y\log(\hat{y}) + (1-y)\log(1-\hat{y})\right]
\]

So the loss is a function of a function of a function.
Because of this layered dependency, we must apply the chain rule when computing gradients.

title: 7  Backpropagation for Classification
section: 7.7 Step 4: the chain rule for this model
text:
7.7 Step 4: the chain rule for this model
Suppose we want the gradient with respect to \(w_1\).
A change in \(w_1\):

changes \(z\),
which changes \(\hat{y}\),
which changes the loss \(L\).

Therefore:
\[
\frac{\partial L}{\partial w_1}
=
\frac{\partial L}{\partial \hat{y}}
\cdot
\frac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial z}
\cdot
\frac{\partial z}{\partial w_1}
\]
Each factor represents one link in the dependency chain.
We now compute these pieces one by one.

title: 7  Backpropagation for Classification
section: 7.8 Step 5: derivative of the loss with respect to the prediction
text:
7.8 Step 5: derivative of the loss with respect to the prediction
Start from the binary cross-entropy definition:
\[
L = -\left[y \log(\hat{y}) + (1-y)\log(1-\hat{y})\right]
\]
Differentiate with respect to \(\hat{y}\):
\[
\frac{\partial L}{\partial \hat{y}}
=
-\left(\frac{y}{\hat{y}} - \frac{1-y}{1-\hat{y}}\right)
\]
For this example the true target is:
\[
y = 1
\]
So the formula simplifies to:
\[
\frac{\partial L}{\partial \hat{y}} = -\frac{1}{\hat{y}}
\]
Substitute the prediction:
\[
\hat{y} \approx 0.8176
\]
Then:
\[
\frac{\partial L}{\partial \hat{y}} \approx -\frac{1}{0.8176}
\]
\[
\frac{\partial L}{\partial \hat{y}} \approx -1.2231
\]

title: 7  Backpropagation for Classification
text:
7.9 Why this sign makes sense
The derivative is negative because the target is \(1\) but the prediction is still below \(1\).
So if \(\hat{y}\) increases a little, the loss should decrease.
That is why the slope with respect to \(\hat{y}\) is negative here.

title: 7  Backpropagation for Classification
section: 7.10 Step 6: derivative of the sigmoid output with respect to the score
text:
7.10 Step 6: derivative of the sigmoid output with respect to the score
The sigmoid function is:
\[
\hat{y} = \sigma(z)
\]
A useful property of sigmoid is that its derivative has a simple form:
\[
\frac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial z} = \hat{y}(1-\hat{y})
\]
Substitute the prediction:
\[
\hat{y} \approx 0.8176
\]
Compute:
\[
\frac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial z}
\approx 0.8176(1-0.8176)
\]
\[
\frac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial z}
\approx 0.8176(0.1824)
\]
\[
\frac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial z} \approx 0.1491
\]
So:
\[
\frac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial z} \approx 0.1491
\]

title: 7  Backpropagation for Classification
text:
7.11 Why this derivative matters
This derivative tells us how sensitive the prediction is to the score \(z\).
If this value is small, then even a noticeable change in \(z\) produces only a small change in \(\hat{y}\).
That is why sigmoid can sometimes learn slowly when it saturates near 0 or 1.

title: 7  Backpropagation for Classification
section: 7.12 Step 7: derivative of the linear score with respect to the parameters
text:
7.12 Step 7: derivative of the linear score with respect to the parameters
The linear score is:
\[
z = w_1 x_1 + w_2 x_2 + b
\]
The derivatives are simple because this expression is linear.

7.12.1 With respect to \(w_1\)
\[
\frac{\partial z}{\partial w_1} = x_1
\]
Given:
\[
x_1 = 1
\]
so:
\[
\frac{\partial z}{\partial w_1} = 1
\]


7.12.2 With respect to \(w_2\)
\[
\frac{\partial z}{\partial w_2} = x_2
\]
Given:
\[
x_2 = 0
\]
so:
\[
\frac{\partial z}{\partial w_2} = 0
\]


7.12.3 With respect to the bias
\[
\frac{\partial z}{\partial b} = 1
\]

title: 7  Backpropagation for Classification
text:
7.13 Why \(\partial z / \partial w_2 = 0\) here
This is an important teaching moment.
The input \(x_2\) for this example is:
\[
x_2 = 0
\]
So no matter what value \(w_2\) has, the product \(w_2 x_2\) contributes nothing to the score for this one training case.
That is why this example gives no update signal for \(w_2\).

title: 7  Backpropagation for Classification
section: 7.14 Step 8: combine the pieces using the chain rule
text:
7.14 Step 8: combine the pieces using the chain rule
Now multiply the derivatives along the chain.

7.14.1 Gradient for \(w_1\)
\[
\frac{\partial L}{\partial w_1}
=
\frac{\partial L}{\partial \hat{y}}
\cdot
\frac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial z}
\cdot
\frac{\partial z}{\partial w_1}
\]
Substitute values:
\[
\frac{\partial L}{\partial w_1}
\approx (-1.2231)(0.1491)(1)
\]
\[
\frac{\partial L}{\partial w_1} \approx -0.1823
\]


7.14.2 Gradient for \(w_2\)
\[
\frac{\partial L}{\partial w_2}
=
\frac{\partial L}{\partial \hat{y}}
\cdot
\frac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial z}
\cdot
\frac{\partial z}{\partial w_2}
\]
Substitute values:
\[
\frac{\partial L}{\partial w_2}
\approx (-1.2231)(0.1491)(0)
\]
\[
\frac{\partial L}{\partial w_2} = 0
\]


7.14.3 Gradient for \(b\)
\[
\frac{\partial L}{\partial b}
=
\frac{\partial L}{\partial \hat{y}}
\cdot
\frac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial z}
\cdot
\frac{\partial z}{\partial b}
\]
Substitute values:
\[
\frac{\partial L}{\partial b}
\approx (-1.2231)(0.1491)(1)
\]
\[
\frac{\partial L}{\partial b} \approx -0.1823
\]
So approximately:
\[
\frac{\partial L}{\partial w_1} \approx -0.1823
\]
\[
\frac{\partial L}{\partial w_2} = 0
\]
\[
\frac{\partial L}{\partial b} \approx -0.1823
\]

title: 7  Backpropagation for Classification
text:
7.15 Step 9: an important simplification
For sigmoid output combined with binary cross-entropy, the gradient simplifies to a very elegant result:
\[
\frac{\partial L}{\partial z} = \hat{y} - y
\]
For sigmoid output with binary cross-entropy loss, this gradient simplification is standard (Bishop, 2006; Goodfellow et al., 2016; Rumelhart et al., 1986).
Let us verify it numerically.
We had:
\[
\hat{y} \approx 0.8176
\]
and:
\[
y = 1
\]
So:
\[
\hat{y} - y = 0.8176 - 1
\]
\[
\hat{y} - y \approx -0.1824
\]
This matches the chain-rule result above up to rounding.
Because of this property, we can write the gradients more simply:
\[
\frac{\partial L}{\partial w_1} = (\hat{y}-y)x_1
\]
\[
\frac{\partial L}{\partial w_2} = (\hat{y}-y)x_2
\]
\[
\frac{\partial L}{\partial b} = \hat{y}-y
\]

title: 7  Backpropagation for Classification
text:
7.16 Why this simplification is powerful
Without simplification, we must compute:

the derivative of the loss with respect to \(\hat{y}\),
the derivative of sigmoid with respect to \(z\),
the derivative of \(z\) with respect to each parameter.

With the simplification, the middle pieces collapse into one neat term:
\[
\hat{y} - y
\]
This makes classification backpropagation much easier to implement and teach.

title: 7  Backpropagation for Classification
text:
7.17 Step 10: update the parameters
Gradient descent updates parameters using:
\[
\theta_{\text{new}} = \theta_{\text{old}} - \eta \frac{\partial L}{\partial \theta}
\]
The learning rate is:
\[
\eta = 0.1
\]
We computed:
\[
\hat{y} - y \approx -0.1824
\]

7.17.1 Update \(w_1\)
\[
w_1^{\text{new}} = 2 - 0.1(-0.1824)
\]
\[
w_1^{\text{new}} = 2 + 0.01824
\]
\[
w_1^{\text{new}} = 2.01824
\]


7.17.2 Update \(w_2\)
\[
w_2^{\text{new}} = -1 - 0.1(0)
\]
\[
w_2^{\text{new}} = -1
\]


7.17.3 Update \(b\)
\[
b^{\text{new}} = -0.5 - 0.1(-0.1824)
\]
\[
b^{\text{new}} = -0.5 + 0.01824
\]
\[
b^{\text{new}} = -0.48176
\]
So the updated parameters are approximately:
\[
w_1 = 2.0182
\]
\[
w_2 = -1.0
\]
\[
b = -0.4818
\]

title: 7  Backpropagation for Classification
section: 7.18 Step 11: check whether the loss decreased
text:
7.18 Step 11: check whether the loss decreased
Now compute the new score:
\[
z_{\text{new}} = (2.01824)(1) + (-1)(0) + (-0.48176)
\]
\[
z_{\text{new}} = 1.53648
\]
Now compute the new prediction:
\[
\hat{y}_{\text{new}} = \sigma(1.53648) \approx 0.8230
\]
Because the true target is 1, the prediction moved slightly closer to 1.
Now compute the new loss:
\[
L_{\text{new}} = -\log(0.8230) \approx 0.1949
\]
So the loss decreased approximately:
\[
0.2014 \to 0.1949
\]

title: 7  Backpropagation for Classification
text:
7.19 Why the loss decreased
The target is:
\[
y = 1
\]
The original prediction was:
\[
0.8176
\]
The new prediction became slightly larger:
\[
0.8230
\]
That moves the model in the correct direction for this example.
So the loss decreases.

title: 7  Backpropagation for Classification
text:
7.20 A small Python check

import math

x1 = 1
x2 = 0
y = 1

w1 = 2.0
w2 = -1.0
b = -0.5
eta = 0.1

def sigmoid(z):
    return 1 / (1 + math.exp(-z))

# Forward pass
z = w1 * x1 + w2 * x2 + b
y_hat = sigmoid(z)
loss = -(y * math.log(y_hat) + (1 - y) * math.log(1 - y_hat))

# Simplified gradient for sigmoid + BCE
dL_dz = y_hat - y
dL_dw1 = dL_dz * x1
dL_dw2 = dL_dz * x2
dL_db = dL_dz

# Update
w1_new = w1 - eta * dL_dw1
w2_new = w2 - eta * dL_dw2
b_new = b - eta * dL_db

# New forward pass
z_new = w1_new * x1 + w2_new * x2 + b_new
y_hat_new = sigmoid(z_new)
loss_new = -(y * math.log(y_hat_new) + (1 - y) * math.log(1 - y_hat_new))

print("Initial z:", round(z, 4))
print("Initial prediction:", round(y_hat, 4))
print("Initial loss:", round(loss, 4))
print()
print("Gradients:")
print("  dL/dz  =", round(dL_dz, 4))
print("  dL/dw1 =", round(dL_dw1, 4))
print("  dL/dw2 =", round(dL_dw2, 4))
print("  dL/db  =", round(dL_db, 4))
print()
print("Updated parameters:")
print("  w1 =", round(w1_new, 4))
print("  w2 =", round(w2_new, 4))
print("  b  =", round(b_new, 4))
print()
print("New z:", round(z_new, 4))
print("New prediction:", round(y_hat_new, 4))
print("New loss:", round(loss_new, 4))

Initial z: 1.5
Initial prediction: 0.8176
Initial loss: 0.2014

Gradients:
  dL/dz  = -0.1824
  dL/dw1 = -0.1824
  dL/dw2 = -0.0
  dL/db  = -0.1824

Updated parameters:
  w1 = 2.0182
  w2 = -1.0
  b  = -0.4818

New z: 1.5365
New prediction: 0.823
New loss: 0.1949

title: 7  Backpropagation for Classification
section: 7.21 Why this chapter matters so much
text:
7.21 Why this chapter matters so much
This chapter shows a very important truth:
Backpropagation is still the chain rule, but some combinations of activation and loss produce elegant simplifications.
For binary classification with sigmoid and cross-entropy:
\[
\frac{\partial L}{\partial z} = \hat{y} - y
\]
That is one of the most useful formulas in introductory neural-network learning.

title: 7  Backpropagation for Classification
text:
7.22 Common mistakes

7.22.1 Mistake 1: differentiating binary cross-entropy incorrectly
The logarithms and signs matter. One missing minus sign changes the result.


7.22.2 Mistake 2: forgetting the sigmoid derivative
If you do the long chain-rule route, you must include:
\[
\hat{y}(1-\hat{y})
\]


7.22.3 Mistake 3: forgetting the simplification applies to this specific pairing
The simplification:
\[
\frac{\partial L}{\partial z} = \hat{y} - y
\]
depends on using sigmoid output together with binary cross-entropy.
It is not a universal formula for every activation-loss combination.


7.22.4 Mistake 4: being confused by \(w_2\) having zero gradient here
That happened only because:
\[
x_2 = 0
\]
So this example gives no immediate update signal for \(w_2\).

title: 7  Backpropagation for Classification
text:
7.23 Chapter checklist
By the end of this chapter, you should be able to answer:

What is the computation chain in classification backpropagation?
Why do we use sigmoid here?
What is binary cross-entropy measuring?
Why does the gradient simplify to \(\hat{y} - y\)?
Why was \(\frac{\partial L}{\partial w_2} = 0\) in this example?
Why did the loss decrease after the update?

title: 9  Solutions to Suggested Exercises
section: 9.3 Solutions to suggested by-hand exercises from earlier chapters
text:
9.3 Solutions to suggested by-hand exercises from earlier chapters


9.3.1 Solution S1: Basics of Neural Networks — suggested by-hand exercise
Question source: basics-of-neural-networks.qmd, section Suggested by-hand exercise
Given
\[
x_1 = 1,\quad x_2 = 4,\quad w_1 = 2,\quad w_2 = -0.5,\quad b = 1
\]
We use the single-neuron formula:
\[
z = w_1 x_1 + w_2 x_2 + b
\]
The exercise asks for:

the pre-activation value \(z\),
the output using identity activation,
the output using sigmoid activation.


9.3.1.1 Step 1: compute the pre-activation value \(z\)
Start with the formula:
\[
z = w_1 x_1 + w_2 x_2 + b
\]
Substitute the given values:
\[
z = (2)(1) + (-0.5)(4) + 1
\]
Compute each term separately:
\[
(2)(1) = 2
\]
\[
(-0.5)(4) = -2
\]
Now substitute those results back in:
\[
z = 2 + (-2) + 1
\]
\[
z = 0 + 1
\]
\[
z = 1
\]
So the pre-activation value is:
\[
z = 1
\]


9.3.1.2 Step 2: compute the output using identity activation
The identity activation is:
\[
f(z) = z
\]
So the output is:
\[
a = f(z) = z
\]
We already found:
\[
z = 1
\]
Therefore:
\[
a = 1
\]
So the output using identity activation is:
\[
a = 1
\]


9.3.1.3 Step 3: compute the output using sigmoid activation
The sigmoid activation is:
\[
\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}
\]
Substitute \(z = 1\):
\[
\sigma(1) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-1}}
\]
Use the approximation:
\[
e^{-1} \approx 0.3679
\]
Then:
\[
\sigma(1) \approx \frac{1}{1 + 0.3679}
\]
\[
\sigma(1) \approx \frac{1}{1.3679}
\]
\[
\sigma(1) \approx 0.7311
\]
So the output using sigmoid activation is approximately:
\[
\sigma(1) \approx 0.7311
\]


9.3.1.4 Final answers

Pre-activation value:
\[
z = 1
\]
Output using identity activation:
\[
a = 1
\]
Output using sigmoid activation:
\[
\sigma(1) \approx 0.7311
\]



9.3.1.5 Interpretation
The same pre-activation value \(z = 1\) produces different final outputs depending on the activation function. Identity leaves the value unchanged, while sigmoid transforms it into a number between 0 and 1.

9.3.4 Solution S4: Backpropagation for Regression — suggested by-hand exercise
Question source: backpropagation-1-regression.qmd, section Suggested by-hand exercise
Given
\[
x_1 = 2,\quad x_2 = 1,\quad y = 5
\]
Initial parameters:
\[
w_1 = 0.5,\quad w_2 = 1.0,\quad b = 0.0
\]
Learning rate:
\[
\eta = 0.1
\]
The model is:
\[
\hat{y} = w_1 x_1 + w_2 x_2 + b
\]
The activation function is the identity function:
\[
f(z) = z
\]
The loss is:
\[
L = \frac{1}{2}(y - \hat{y})^2
\]
The exercise asks for:

the prediction \(\hat{y}\),
the loss \(L\),
\(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \hat{y}}\),
\(\frac{\partial L}{\partial w_1}\),
\(\frac{\partial L}{\partial w_2}\),
\(\frac{\partial L}{\partial b}\),
the updated parameters,
the new prediction and new loss.


9.3.4.1 Step 1: compute the prediction
Use:
\[
\hat{y} = w_1 x_1 + w_2 x_2 + b
\]
Substitute the given values:
\[
\hat{y} = (0.5)(2) + (1.0)(1) + 0.0
\]
Compute each term:
\[
(0.5)(2) = 1.0
\]
\[
(1.0)(1) = 1.0
\]
So:
\[
\hat{y} = 1.0 + 1.0 + 0.0 = 2.0
\]
Therefore:
\[
\hat{y} = 2.0
\]


9.3.4.2 Step 2: compute the loss
Use:
\[
L = \frac{1}{2}(y - \hat{y})^2
\]
Substitute \(y = 5\) and \(\hat{y} = 2.0\):
\[
L = \frac{1}{2}(5 - 2.0)^2
\]
Compute the error:
\[
5 - 2.0 = 3.0
\]
Square it:
\[
(3.0)^2 = 9.0
\]
Multiply by \(\frac{1}{2}\):
\[
L = \frac{1}{2}(9.0) = 4.5
\]
Therefore:
\[
L = 4.5
\]


9.3.4.3 Step 3: compute \(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \hat{y}}\)
Start from:
\[
L = \frac{1}{2}(y - \hat{y})^2
\]
For this loss:
\[
\frac{\partial L}{\partial \hat{y}} = \hat{y} - y
\]
Substitute the values:
\[
\frac{\partial L}{\partial \hat{y}} = 2.0 - 5
\]
\[
\frac{\partial L}{\partial \hat{y}} = -3.0
\]


9.3.4.4 Step 4: compute the derivatives of \(\hat{y}\) with respect to the parameters
Because:
\[
\hat{y} = w_1 x_1 + w_2 x_2 + b
\]
we have:
\[
\frac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial w_1} = x_1,\quad
\frac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial w_2} = x_2,\quad
\frac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial b} = 1
\]
Substitute the input values:
\[
\frac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial w_1} = 2
\]
\[
\frac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial w_2} = 1
\]
\[
\frac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial b} = 1
\]


9.3.4.5 Step 5: compute the gradients
Use the chain rule:
\[
\frac{\partial L}{\partial w_1}
=
\frac{\partial L}{\partial \hat{y}}
\cdot
\frac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial w_1}
\]
\[
\frac{\partial L}{\partial w_2}
=
\frac{\partial L}{\partial \hat{y}}
\cdot
\frac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial w_2}
\]
\[
\frac{\partial L}{\partial b}
=
\frac{\partial L}{\partial \hat{y}}
\cdot
\frac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial b}
\]

9.3.4.5.1 Gradient for \(w_1\)
\[
\frac{\partial L}{\partial w_1} = (-3.0)(2)
\]
\[
\frac{\partial L}{\partial w_1} = -6.0
\]


9.3.4.5.2 Gradient for \(w_2\)
\[
\frac{\partial L}{\partial w_2} = (-3.0)(1)
\]
\[
\frac{\partial L}{\partial w_2} = -3.0
\]


9.3.4.5.3 Gradient for \(b\)
\[
\frac{\partial L}{\partial b} = (-3.0)(1)
\]
\[
\frac{\partial L}{\partial b} = -3.0
\]
So the gradients are:
\[
\frac{\partial L}{\partial w_1} = -6.0,\quad
\frac{\partial L}{\partial w_2} = -3.0,\quad
\frac{\partial L}{\partial b} = -3.0
\]



9.3.4.6 Step 6: update the parameters
Use gradient descent:
\[
\theta_{\text{new}} = \theta_{\text{old}} - \eta \frac{\partial L}{\partial \theta}
\]
with:
\[
\eta = 0.1
\]

9.3.4.6.1 Update \(w_1\)
\[
w_1^{\text{new}} = 0.5 - 0.1(-6.0)
\]
\[
w_1^{\text{new}} = 0.5 + 0.6
\]
\[
w_1^{\text{new}} = 1.1
\]


9.3.4.6.2 Update \(w_2\)
\[
w_2^{\text{new}} = 1.0 - 0.1(-3.0)
\]
\[
w_2^{\text{new}} = 1.0 + 0.3
\]
\[
w_2^{\text{new}} = 1.3
\]


9.3.4.6.3 Update \(b\)
\[
b^{\text{new}} = 0.0 - 0.1(-3.0)
\]
\[
b^{\text{new}} = 0.0 + 0.3
\]
\[
b^{\text{new}} = 0.3
\]
So the updated parameters are:
\[
w_1 = 1.1,\quad w_2 = 1.3,\quad b = 0.3
\]



9.3.4.7 Step 7: compute the new prediction
Use the updated parameters:
\[
\hat{y}_{\text{new}} = w_1^{\text{new}} x_1 + w_2^{\text{new}} x_2 + b^{\text{new}}
\]
Substitute the values:
\[
\hat{y}_{\text{new}} = (1.1)(2) + (1.3)(1) + 0.3
\]
Compute each term:
\[
(1.1)(2) = 2.2
\]
\[
(1.3)(1) = 1.3
\]
So:
\[
\hat{y}_{\text{new}} = 2.2 + 1.3 + 0.3
\]
\[
\hat{y}_{\text{new}} = 3.8
\]
Therefore:
\[
\hat{y}_{\text{new}} = 3.8
\]


9.3.4.8 Step 8: compute the new loss
Use:
\[
L_{\text{new}} = \frac{1}{2}(y - \hat{y}_{\text{new}})^2
\]
Substitute \(y = 5\) and \(\hat{y}_{\text{new}} = 3.8\):
\[
L_{\text{new}} = \frac{1}{2}(5 - 3.8)^2
\]
Compute the new error:
\[
5 - 3.8 = 1.2
\]
Square it:
\[
(1.2)^2 = 1.44
\]
Multiply by \(\frac{1}{2}\):
\[
L_{\text{new}} = \frac{1}{2}(1.44) = 0.72
\]
Therefore:
\[
L_{\text{new}} = 0.72
\]


9.3.4.9 Final answers

Initial prediction:
\[
\hat{y} = 2.0
\]
Initial loss:
\[
L = 4.5
\]
Derivative with respect to prediction:
\[
\frac{\partial L}{\partial \hat{y}} = -3.0
\]
Gradients:
\[
\frac{\partial L}{\partial w_1} = -6.0,\quad
\frac{\partial L}{\partial w_2} = -3.0,\quad
\frac{\partial L}{\partial b} = -3.0
\]
Updated parameters:
\[
w_1 = 1.1,\quad w_2 = 1.3,\quad b = 0.3
\]
New prediction:
\[
\hat{y}_{\text{new}} = 3.8
\]
New loss:
\[
L_{\text{new}} = 0.72
\]



9.3.4.10 Interpretation
The initial prediction was far below the target, so the gradients were negative. After subtracting those gradients, the parameters increased, the prediction moved closer to the target, and the loss dropped from \(4.5\) to \(0.72\).



9.3.5 Solution S5: Backpropagation for Classification — suggested by-hand exercise
Question source: backpropagation-2-classification.qmd, section Suggested by-hand exercise
Given
\[
x_1 = 1,\quad x_2 = 1,\quad y = 1
\]
Initial parameters:
\[
w_1 = 2,\quad w_2 = -1,\quad b = -0.5
\]
Learning rate:
\[
\eta = 0.1
\]
The model is:
\[
z = w_1 x_1 + w_2 x_2 + b
\]
\[
\hat{y} = \sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}
\]
The loss is binary cross-entropy:
\[
L = -\left[y \log(\hat{y}) + (1-y)\log(1-\hat{y})\right]
\]
For sigmoid output with binary cross-entropy, we use the simplification:
\[
\frac{\partial L}{\partial z} = \hat{y} - y
\]
The exercise asks for:

\(z\),
\(\hat{y}\),
the loss,
\(\hat{y} - y\),
the gradients for \(w_1\), \(w_2\), and \(b\),
the updated parameters,
the new prediction and new loss.


9.3.5.1 Step 1: compute the score \(z\)
Use:
\[
z = w_1 x_1 + w_2 x_2 + b
\]
Substitute the given values:
\[
z = (2)(1) + (-1)(1) + (-0.5)
\]
Compute each term:
\[
(2)(1) = 2
\]
\[
(-1)(1) = -1
\]
Now add them:
\[
z = 2 + (-1) + (-0.5)
\]
\[
z = 1 - 0.5
\]
\[
z = 0.5
\]
So:
\[
z = 0.5
\]


9.3.5.2 Step 2: compute the prediction \(\hat{y}\)
Use the sigmoid formula:
\[
\hat{y} = \sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}
\]
Substitute \(z = 0.5\):
\[
\hat{y} = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-0.5}}
\]
Use the approximation:
\[
e^{-0.5} \approx 0.6065
\]
Then:
\[
\hat{y} \approx \frac{1}{1 + 0.6065}
\]
\[
\hat{y} \approx \frac{1}{1.6065}
\]
\[
\hat{y} \approx 0.6225
\]
So:
\[
\hat{y} \approx 0.6225
\]


9.3.5.3 Step 3: compute the loss
The target is:
\[
y = 1
\]
So binary cross-entropy simplifies to:
\[
L = -\log(\hat{y})
\]
Substitute \(\hat{y} \approx 0.6225\):
\[
L \approx -\log(0.6225)
\]
Use the approximation:
\[
\log(0.6225) \approx -0.4741
\]
Therefore:
\[
L \approx 0.4741
\]
So:
\[
L \approx 0.4741
\]


9.3.5.4 Step 4: compute \(\hat{y} - y\)
Use:
\[
\hat{y} - y
\]
Substitute the values:
\[
\hat{y} - y \approx 0.6225 - 1
\]
\[
\hat{y} - y \approx -0.3775
\]
So:
\[
\hat{y} - y \approx -0.3775
\]


9.3.5.5 Step 5: compute the gradients
Because:
\[
\frac{\partial L}{\partial z} = \hat{y} - y
\]
and:
\[
z = w_1 x_1 + w_2 x_2 + b
\]
the gradients are:
\[
\frac{\partial L}{\partial w_1} = (\hat{y} - y)x_1
\]
\[
\frac{\partial L}{\partial w_2} = (\hat{y} - y)x_2
\]
\[
\frac{\partial L}{\partial b} = \hat{y} - y
\]
Substitute the values \(x_1 = 1\), \(x_2 = 1\), and \(\hat{y} - y \approx -0.3775\).

9.3.5.5.1 Gradient for \(w_1\)
\[
\frac{\partial L}{\partial w_1} \approx (-0.3775)(1)
\]
\[
\frac{\partial L}{\partial w_1} \approx -0.3775
\]


9.3.5.5.2 Gradient for \(w_2\)
\[
\frac{\partial L}{\partial w_2} \approx (-0.3775)(1)
\]
\[
\frac{\partial L}{\partial w_2} \approx -0.3775
\]


9.3.5.5.3 Gradient for \(b\)
\[
\frac{\partial L}{\partial b} \approx -0.3775
\]
So the gradients are:
\[
\frac{\partial L}{\partial w_1} \approx -0.3775,\quad
\frac{\partial L}{\partial w_2} \approx -0.3775,\quad
\frac{\partial L}{\partial b} \approx -0.3775
\]



9.3.5.6 Step 6: update the parameters
Use gradient descent:
\[
\theta_{\text{new}} = \theta_{\text{old}} - \eta \frac{\partial L}{\partial \theta}
\]
with:
\[
\eta = 0.1
\]

9.3.5.6.1 Update \(w_1\)
\[
w_1^{\text{new}} = 2 - 0.1(-0.3775)
\]
\[
w_1^{\text{new}} = 2 + 0.03775
\]
\[
w_1^{\text{new}} = 2.03775
\]


9.3.5.6.2 Update \(w_2\)
\[
w_2^{\text{new}} = -1 - 0.1(-0.3775)
\]
\[
w_2^{\text{new}} = -1 + 0.03775
\]
\[
w_2^{\text{new}} = -0.96225
\]


9.3.5.6.3 Update \(b\)
\[
b^{\text{new}} = -0.5 - 0.1(-0.3775)
\]
\[
b^{\text{new}} = -0.5 + 0.03775
\]
\[
b^{\text{new}} = -0.46225
\]
So the updated parameters are:
\[
w_1 \approx 2.03775,\quad w_2 \approx -0.96225,\quad b \approx -0.46225
\]



9.3.5.7 Step 7: compute the new score and new prediction
Use the updated parameters:
\[
z_{\text{new}} = w_1^{\text{new}} x_1 + w_2^{\text{new}} x_2 + b^{\text{new}}
\]
Substitute the values:
\[
z_{\text{new}} = (2.03775)(1) + (-0.96225)(1) + (-0.46225)
\]
Compute step by step:
\[
z_{\text{new}} = 2.03775 - 0.96225 - 0.46225
\]
\[
z_{\text{new}} = 1.0755 - 0.46225
\]
\[
z_{\text{new}} = 0.61325
\]
So:
\[
z_{\text{new}} = 0.61325
\]
Now compute the new prediction:
\[
\hat{y}_{\text{new}} = \sigma(z_{\text{new}}) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-0.61325}}
\]
Use the approximation:
\[
e^{-0.61325} \approx 0.5416
\]
Then:
\[
\hat{y}_{\text{new}} \approx \frac{1}{1 + 0.5416}
\]
\[
\hat{y}_{\text{new}} \approx \frac{1}{1.5416}
\]
\[
\hat{y}_{\text{new}} \approx 0.6487
\]
So:
\[
\hat{y}_{\text{new}} \approx 0.6487
\]


9.3.5.8 Step 8: compute the new loss
Because \(y = 1\), the new loss is:
\[
L_{\text{new}} = -\log(\hat{y}_{\text{new}})
\]
Substitute \(\hat{y}_{\text{new}} \approx 0.6487\):
\[
L_{\text{new}} \approx -\log(0.6487)
\]
Use the approximation:
\[
\log(0.6487) \approx -0.4328
\]
Therefore:
\[
L_{\text{new}} \approx 0.4328
\]
So:
\[
L_{\text{new}} \approx 0.4328
\]


9.3.5.9 Final answers

Initial score:
\[
z = 0.5
\]
Initial prediction:
\[
\hat{y} \approx 0.6225
\]
Initial loss:
\[
L \approx 0.4741
\]
Simplified error term:
\[
\hat{y} - y \approx -0.3775
\]
Gradients:
\[
\frac{\partial L}{\partial w_1} \approx -0.3775,\quad
\frac{\partial L}{\partial w_2} \approx -0.3775,\quad
\frac{\partial L}{\partial b} \approx -0.3775
\]
Updated parameters:
\[
w_1 \approx 2.03775,\quad
w_2 \approx -0.96225,\quad
b \approx -0.46225
\]
New prediction:
\[
\hat{y}_{\text{new}} \approx 0.6487
\]
New loss:
\[
L_{\text{new}} \approx 0.4328
\]



9.3.5.10 Interpretation
The target is 1, so the model should push its prediction upward. Because \(\hat{y} - y\) is negative, the parameter updates increase the score \(z\), which increases the sigmoid output and lowers the loss.
